segments.s452-g57: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense verb 'he commanded/said' (subject implied). تا here introduces the subordinate/temporal clause ('when/as soon as') and links بفرمود to the following clause. جامه‌ها خواستند is a simple past clause 'they requested/wanted the garments' (خواستند = past of خواستن).
segments.s452-g58: به is the preposition introducing the location ('to/towards/into'); خرگاه is the place noun 'khar‑gah' (literally a donkey‑stall or lowly stable). جایی is 'a place/spot' and بیاراستند is past plural 'they adorned/arranged' — together 'they set up/ornamented a place in the khar‑gah.'
segments.s452-g59: به کردار here means 'in the manner/appearance of' (an adverbial use of به + کردار). کوه آتشی is a poetic simile 'a mountain of fire' (کوه = mountain; آتشی = fiery). برفروخت is a past verb meaning 'kindled/inflamed' (not 'sold' here).
segments.s452-g60: بسی functions as an adverb/quantifier 'much/a great deal.' عود is 'agarwood (oud)'; و ... و links the items: 'and with musk and amber(gris)'. با marks accompaniment ('with'). بسوخت is a passive/impersonal past 'was burned/burned up.'
segments.s452-g61: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' خورشید 'the sun' is the subject; سر برزد is a poetic expression 'raises its head/appears' (سر = head; برزد is an archaic verb meaning 'came/appeared' here). از کوهسار = 'from/out of the mountain ridge.'
segments.s452-g62: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed (general)'. برفتن is an infinitive 'to depart' indicating the intended action. آراست is a past tense verb 'he arranged/prepared' with بر functioning as a prefixed preposition/particle attached to the verb phrase; کار is the object 'affairs/work.' The whole means he prepared to leave and set his affairs in order.
segments.s452-g63: بفرمود 'he commanded' again; تا introduces the subordinate clause/purpose. موبدی 'a mobad' (Zoroastrian priest) is the noun in apposition and رهنمای is the predicative noun 'guide' — together 'he ordered a mobad to be (serve as) guide.'
segments.s452-g64: یکی دست جامه is an idiomatic quantifier 'one set/suit of clothes' (یکی = one; دست جامه = a full outfit). ز سر تا به پای is the fixed phrase 'from head to foot' with ز = 'from,' تا = 'up to/until,' به = directional 'to,' and پای = 'foot.' Together: 'one full suit of clothes from head to toe.'
segments.s452-g81: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing three definite people. 'زن گازر' is a juxtaposition where the noun 'زن' (woman/wife) precedes the name 'گازر' to mean 'the wife of Gāzar' (classical Persian often omits the ezāfe linking element). The two conjunctions 'و' simply join the three items ('the wife of Gāzar', 'Gāzar', 'Mehré'). The particle 'را' at the end is the definite/object marker, here marking the whole listed group as the (definite) object of the verb that follows in the next segment.
segments.s452-g82: 'بیارید' is a 2nd-person plural/formal imperative meaning 'bring' (classical/poetic form corresponding to modern بیاورید). The verb comes before its objects here; the following coordination 'بهرام و هم زهره را' names two more definite objects. 'هم' functions as 'also/too', and the final 'را' again marks the definite object(s). Together this clause issues the command to bring Bahram and Zohreh in addition to the previously listed people.
segments.s452-g1: چنان بد که is a fixed concessive phrase meaning “so/so bad that,” where چنان (so) modifies بد (bad). که here is the conjunction “that” introducing the following clause 'روزی یکی تندباد' = 'one day a gale'. Note the word order: adjective phrase (چنان بد) + که + temporal/indefinite subject clause.
segments.s452-g2: برآمد is a past action verb “arose/issued forth.” غمی گشت is a small clause where غمی (grief) is the predicate that ‘became’ (گشت = became). زان indicates cause (“from that / because of that”) and رشنواد is a proper name placed at the end as the logical subject/experiencer: ‘Rashnawad became sorrowful from that.’
segments.s452-g3: یکی here introduces the scene (‘there was / a certain’). The noun phrase lists storm elements: رعد و باران (thunder and rain) with (با) برق (lightning) و جوش (and surge/turbulence). The coordination and با mark accompaniment/contents of the storm.
segments.s452-g4: This is two nominal descriptions: زمین پر ز آب = ‘the earth full of water’ (زمین + پر 'full' + ز 'of/from' + آب), and آسمان پرخروش = ‘the sky very turbulent’ (پرخروش is a fused adjective meaning full of tumult). Adjective phrases follow their nouns in this poetic order.
segments.s452-g5: به هر سو = ‘to every side/toward every direction.’ ز باران sets the cause ‘because of the rain.’ همی is the archaic/poetic progressive marker (indicating ongoing action) and تاختند is a past plural verb ‘they rushed/charged.’ The clause thus means ‘they rushed off in all directions because of the rain.’
segments.s452-g6: به دشت اندرون = ‘into/unto the plain’s interior’ (به + noun + اندرون). خیمه‌ها ساختند is a past plural action ‘they made/erected tents’ (ساختند). The verb applies to the plural subject implied in the previous clause.
segments.s452-g7: غمی بود زان کار is a complete clause: غمی (grief) + بود (was) + زان کار (from/because of that affair) — ‘there was grief because of that matter.’ داراب نیز appended here marks that Darab is also involved (‘Darab too’), preparing the subject for the next verb.
segments.s452-g8: ز باران = ‘from/because of the rain.’ همی again is the progressive/iterative marker. جست (past of جستن) here means ‘leapt / sprang’ or figuratively ‘sought’; followed by راه گریز (‘a way/path of escape’). The whole clause reads ‘(he) sprang/searched for a way of escape from the rain.’
segments.s452-g65: یکی اسپ = 'a certain horse / a horse' (یکی as an indefinite/article). با introduces accompaniment: با زین و زرین ستام = 'with a saddle and golden [stam/trappings]'; Persian lists companions after بَـ/با. زرین is an adjective modifying ستام; word order here places the nouns then the adjective phrase. ستام is an older/obscure word used as a noun here (poetic vocabulary).
segments.s452-g66: کمندی و تیغی = two nominative items: 'a lariat (lasso) and a sword' (the -ی ending marks indefiniteness). به زرین نیام uses به + noun to indicate 'in/with (a) golden sheath': نیام = scabbard/sheath; زرین qualifies it. The conjunction و links the two items to the preceding nominal list (these are gear belonging to the same owner).
segments.s452-g67: به داراب دادند = 'they gave/delivered (him) to Darab' — به marks the indirect object ('to Darab'), دادند is the past tense (3rd person plural) 'they gave/delivered'. و پرسید زوی connects the next clause: پرسید (he/they) asked, and زوی = 'from him' (ز + او contracted), marking the source/agent of the answer.
segments.s452-g68: که‌ای ... نامجوی is an interrogative/vocative formula: که‌ای here functions as 'who are you?' (poetic contraction of 'که ای' or 'کی هستی'). شیردل مهتر = epithets placed before the name: 'lion‑hearted chief' (adjective+noun). نامجوی is the personal name being addressed. The whole is direct speech embedded after پرسید.
segments.s452-g69: چو مردی تو = 'if you are a man' — چو introduces a conditional 'if'; مردی is a predicate noun 'a man' and تو is the subject 'you' (classical word order allows the predicate before the verb 'to be'). و زادبومت کجاست = 'and where is your birthplace/homeland?' زادبومت is the possessive construction 'your homeland' (زادبوم + ـت), کجاست is the copular question 'where is it?'.
segments.s452-g70: سزد‌گر بگویی = 'if it is fitting (سزدگر) you should say / tell (بگویی)'; سزدگر is an impersonal/fixed expression meaning 'if it is fitting' or 'it is proper that...', and بگویی is present/subjunctive-like (classical usage) meaning 'you say' or 'you would tell'. همه راه راست = 'the whole/entire way straight' used idiomatically as 'the whole truth / plainly all of it' (همه modifies راه, راست qualifies it as 'true/straight').
segments.s452-g71: چو بشنید داراب = 'when Darab heard' — چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; بشنید is a past-tense verb 'he heard' (classical past), and داراب is the subject. یکسر بگفت = 'he at once said/spoke' — یکسر is an adverb 'at once/entirely', بگفت is past 'he said' (the prefix ب here is the classical past marker vestige in narrative speech).
segments.s452-g72: گذشته همی برگشاد از نهفت = 'he opened/revealed the past from the hidden' i.e. 'revealed what had been hidden of the past'. گذشته = 'the past' (topic), همی is the classical continuous/emphatic particle often used with past narration, برگشاد is a past-tense verb 'opened/revealed' (برگشاد = بر + گشاد), and از نهفت = 'from what was hidden' (از + نهفت as a noun/adjective 'hidden'). The phrase means he disclosed previously concealed matters.
segments.s452-g33: به فرزانه گفت is the reporting clause: به + indirect object (فرزانه) = 'to the sage', and گفت is the past tense verb 'said'. After گفت comes direct discourse introduced by این چه شاید بدن ('what is this? perhaps a body'), where این چه is a question phrase ('what is this') and شاید is a parenthetical modal ('perhaps').
segments.s452-g34: یکی را سوی طاق باید شدن is a verbless nominal clause with necessity: یکی را (indefinite someone marked by را) is the patient/thing considered, سوی طاق 'toward the arch' gives direction, and باید شدن uses باید + شدن to express necessity/passive result ('must be turned / must become so'). The verb شدن comes after باید, forming the necessity construction.
segments.s452-g35: ببینید is the 2nd-person-plural (or polite) imperative 'look/see'. تا here introduces the clause of specification ('to find out / so that'), so the sequence means 'look to see...'. اندرو خفته کیست is the embedded clause 'who is sleeping in there' with خفته (past participle/adjective 'asleep') and the copula ست at the end.
segments.s452-g36: چنین is an adverb/adjective 'thus/so'. بر تن خود is a locative/possessive phrase 'upon his own body' (بر + تن + خود). برآشفته is a bound participle/adjective meaning 'agitated / dishevelled' that functions as the predicate; کیست again is the copular question 'who is (so)'. Word order places the predicate adjective after the phrase it qualifies.
segments.s452-g37: برفتند is the past tense plural 'they went/proceeded', followed by و 'and' and دیدند 'they saw' (past plural). مردی جوان is an indefinite NP 'a young man' (مردی with the adjectival جوان after the noun, the usual Persian adjective order).
segments.s452-g38: خردمند و با چهره پهلوان are post-nominal modifiers describing that man: خردمند 'wise' is an adjective, followed by و 'and' and a prepositional phrase با چهره پهلوان ('with the face/appearance of a champion'), where با introduces the manner/appearance and چهره پهلوان is a noun phrase 'the face of a hero'.
segments.s452-g39: همه جامه و باره و‌تر و تباه lists the affected items and their state: همه جامه و باره 'all his clothes and baggage' (همه quantifies the following nouns), then و‌تر و تباه gives two coordinating predicative adjectives 'wet (تر) and ruined (تباه)'. The compound is poetic; conjunctions (و) link the items and the adjectives.
segments.s452-g40: ز خاک سیه ساخته جایگاه is a verbless descriptive clause: ز خاک سیه 'from/with black dust' (ز = 'from/of', خاک سیه = 'black dust/earth') modifies ساخته جایگاه 'the place made' (ساخته = 'made/formed', جایگاه = 'place/spot'), i.e. 'the place was formed of black dust'.
segments.s452-g9: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: نگه (gaze/look) + کرد (past of کردن) = 'he looked/gazed.' The phrase ویران یکی جای دید is an inverted noun phrase where ویران (ruined) modifies جای (place), with یکی functioning as an indefinite 'a certain' before جای; دید (saw) is the final past-tense verb (SOV order).
segments.s452-g10: میانش = میان + enclitic -ش, ‘in its middle / in it’; یکی introduces the indefinite noun طاق (an arch). بر پای is a prepositional phrase 'upon a foot/base' (بر + پای) and دید is the concluding past verb 'he saw.' Word order is idiomatic SOV with modifiers before the head noun.
segments.s452-g11: بلند و کهن بود is a pair of adjectival predicates linked by و, with بود (past copula) following the adjectives to state 'it was tall and old.' The clause then repeats و آزرده بود — a parallel structure emphasizing a second adjectival predicate 'it was damaged/distressed.' Repetition of بود after the second adjective is a simple past-tense copula.
segments.s452-g12: یکی خسروی جای is 'a certain royal place' — یکی functions as an indefinite article, خسروی (royal/kingly) modifies جای (place). پر پرده is a genitival/attributive phrase 'full of curtains' (پر = full (of) placed before پرده). The clause ends with بود, the past copula.
segments.s452-g13: نه ... نه is the standard correlative negation 'neither ... nor.' خرگاه بودش places the copula with an enclitic -ش ('it was') after خرگاه (donkey-stall/lowly place), then continues با نه پرده‌سرای to negate the second possibility (پرده‌سرای = curtain-house/theatre). The construction denies that the place is any of these types.
segments.s452-g14: Continuation of the negated list: نه خیمه (neither a tent) نه انباز (nor anbaz — an older/uncertain term listed here) و نه چارپای (and not a four-footed animal). The series is a simple enumeration of things the place is not.
segments.s452-g15: بران (thereon/on that) + طاق آزرده (the ruined arch) forms the locative phrase. بایست is here best read as a past (stative/instantaneous) 'he stopped/stood (there)' rather than modal; it precedes خفت (slept), so together they mean 'he halted there and slept.' The main action culminates in خفت (past 'slept').
segments.s452-g16: چو here functions causally ('since/as/because'), introducing the reason: تنها تنی بود = 'he was a single/solitary body' (تنها 'alone', تنی 'a single person/one body'), followed by the descriptive negatives بی‌یار و جفت ('without a friend/companion and without a mate'). بود is the past copula linking the predicate to the subject.
segments.s452-g49: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. سالار شاه is a doubled honorific noun phrase (literally 'lord / master — king') functioning as the subject; Persian often stacks titles before the verb. آن شگفتی is the direct object ('that marvel') placed before the verb, and بدید is the 3rd-person past form 'he saw.'
segments.s452-g50: سرو پای is an adjectival epithet (literally 'cypress-footed'), with the modifier preceding the noun; داراب را is the definite object marked by را. بنگرید (classical form of نگرِد) here functions as a 3rd-person past/punctual verb 'he looked / he gazed.'
segments.s452-g51: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus' modifying گفت). کاینت is a demonstrative/exclamatory 'this/that' (poetic), followed by the noun شگفتی 'wonder' and the adjective شگفت 'wondrous' used as an emphatic adjective — a brief exclamatory phrase ('O this wonder, wondrous!').
segments.s452-g52: کزین = 'from this' (from + demonstrative). برتر 'higher/superior' modifies اندیشه 'thought' (compound meaning 'a higher thought'). نتوان is the negative potential 'cannot' (present/past-modal in classical style), and گرفت is the past form of گرفتن 'to take/seize' used here figuratively: 'thought cannot seize/conceive anything superior.'
segments.s452-g53: بشد تیز = a classical phrasing meaning 'he became quick' / 'he hurried' (بشد = 'it became / he became'); تیز is an adverb/adjective 'swift.' با او 'with him' (prepositional phrase), به پرده‌سرای 'into/at the curtain-chamber' (به = to/into + پرده‌سرای, a chamber behind curtains or inner apartment).
segments.s452-g54: همی گفت is a continuous/habitual marker + verb 'kept saying' or 'repeatedly said' (همی = still/continually; گفت here functions as 'said'). کای is the vocative 'O'; دادگر is an epithet meaning 'just' or 'just ruler' addressed to the addressee; خدای is 'God.' Together they form an exclamation/vocative ('O just God!').
segments.s452-g55: کسی ... ندید is a negative statement: کسی here functions as 'no one' because followed by the negative verb ندید. در جهان 'in the world' sets the scope; این شگفتی is the object 'this marvel'; ندید is the past negative 'did not see.'
segments.s452-g56: نه ... شنید continues the negation in parallel ('nor did [anyone] hear'). نه introduces the negative alternative. از کار = 'from/of the deed(s)'; دیده is the past-participial/attributive 'seen' (here implying 'in the deeds known/seen'), بزرگان 'the great ones' (former great men/ancestors), and شنید is the verb 'heard/of' (here 'heard of / learned about'). The whole clause means 'nor heard of it among the deeds of the great.'
segments.s452-g17: سپهبد is the subject (spahbed, a military commander). همی is the older/perhaps poetic progressive/habitual particle that marks the action as actual/past-continuous (often: 'was/then'), placed before the verbal phrase. گرد لشکر بگشت is a verb phrase: گرد here functions as a circumpositional/adverbial 'around' (or 'about'), لشکر is its complement 'the army', and بگشت is a past tense verb (from گشتن) 'he turned/returned/turned about' — the verb comes at the end, as in Persian word order S [adv/obj] V. (The supplied senses for بگشت do not include the verbal sense, so that sense is marked unknown.)
segments.s452-g18: بران is a demonstrative/adverbial 'upon that' / 'thereon' linking to what follows. طاق آزرده is a noun + adjective 'the distressed/damaged arch' (noun before adjective, typical in older Persian). اندر گذشت is a compound of the preposition/locative اندر 'into/through/among' plus گذشت (past of گذشتن) 'he passed', with the verb final.
segments.s452-g19: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. ویران functions as a locative noun 'the ruined place/ruins'. خروشی is the noun 'a roar/noise' that is the theme of آمدش. به گوش آمدش is a common Persian idiom meaning 'reached his ear' (به + گوش 'to the ear' + آمدش 'came to him', with -ش enclitic marking 'to him').
segments.s452-g20: کزان is a relative/adverb 'from which' or 'from that (place)'. سهم جای here functions as a fused locative phrase meaning something like 'that very spot / that portion/place' (سهم literally 'share/portion' used idiomatically with جای 'place' to indicate a particular spot). خروش آمدش repeats the idea: 'the cry/roar came to him' (خروش 'cry/roar' + آمدش 'came to him').
segments.s452-g21: که‌ای is an archaic/vocative-like opening (here addressing: 'O ...'); طاق آزرده is the vocative 'distressed arch'. هشیار باش is an imperative: هشیار 'alert/awake' plus the imperative verb باش 'be' — together 'be alert'. Note the adjective-noun order and use of an imperative to give a command.
segments.s452-g22: برین is the fused locution بر + این ('on/for this' → 'this/over this'); شاه ایران is the noun phrase 'king of Iran' with the modifier following the head. نگهدار باش is an imperative construction where نگهدار (literally 'guardian' or 'keeper') functions with باش 'be' to mean 'be (the) guardian/protector'. The whole clause commands: 'Be guardian of this king of Iran.'
segments.s452-g23: نبودش is the negative existential/past 'there was not / he did not have' with the clitic -ش referring to 'him/it'. یکی here means 'a single/any' (emphatic), followed by the list خیمه و یار و جفت 'tent and companion and mate' — a series of nouns joined by و. The phrase states the absence of any tent, friend, or partner.
segments.s452-g24: بیامد is past 'he came'. به زیر تو is a locative/prepositional phrase 'under you / under your (protection)' with زیر 'under' taking تو 'you' as its object. اندر بخفت combines locative اندر 'into/within' with بخفت (past of خوابیدن/خفتن) 'he slept / he lay down', verb-final as usual. Overall: 'he came under you and slept.'
segments.s452-g73: بران سان که is a temporal/relational phrase meaning 'in that way / after that' followed by a subordinate clause introduced by که. آن زن برو کرد is a past clause: برو (go) + کرد (light verb 'did') forms the simple past 'the woman went/left'. یاد here is a noun/gerund 'memory/remembering' that links into the next clause (she, remembering…).
segments.s452-g74: سخنها is the plural object 'words / speeches'. همی + گفت is a poetic/perfective-continuous past sense ('kept saying' / 'was saying'); همی marks iterative or continuous action. با رشنواد means 'to/with Rashnavad' (با marking the addressee/participant).
segments.s452-g75: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. This segment lists items taken out: صندوق 'chest/box' and یاقوت 'rubies'. بازوی خویش = 'her arm' (بازوی + خوی/ش forming the possessive 'خویش' = 'her own'), implying bracelets/arm-jewelry on her arm. The coordinating و connects the listed items.
segments.s452-g76: Again ز marks provenance 'from'. دینار و دیبا are coordinated valuables (dinar coins and brocade/silk). به پهلوی خویش means 'to/at her side' (به + پهلوی 'side'; خویش formed from خوی+ش = 'her own'). The noun phrase modifies where the dinars/diaba are taken or placed.
segments.s452-g77: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one / each in turn'. به سالار لشکر is the indirect object 'to the commander of the army' (preposition به + title + noun). بگفت is the past tense 'said / told' (poetic form with b- prefix). Word order is adverb → indirect object → verb.
segments.s452-g78: A coordinated list introduced by repeated ز ('about/from'): ز خواب و ز آرام و خورد و نهفت enumerates the topics/items she reported: خواب 'sleep', آرام 'rest/quiet', خورد here used as 'eating / food' (from the verb stem), and نهفت 'hiding/being concealed'. The repeated ز is a poetic way to list each item.
segments.s452-g79: هم‌انگه is a temporal adverb 'immediately / at that moment'. فرستاد is the past 'sent / dispatched' (here an action of sending). کس is an indefinite object 'someone', and رشنواد is the person/place connected to the dispatch (read as 'to Rashnavad' or 'for Rashnavad'); classical Persian often omits a dedicated preposition for the recipient.
segments.s452-g80: فرستاده را is 'the messenger' with را marking the (direct/indirect) object or the person addressed. گفت here functions as 'she said / told (him)'. بر سان باد is a fixed optative phrase: بر 'upon', سان 'thus/so', باد is the optative particle 'may it be' — together 'may it be so' or 'let it be thus'.
segments.s452-g25: چنین گفت + با خویشتن + رشنواد: چینیهٔ ابتدایی 'چنین گفت' (past) reports speech and is followed by the reflexive phrase 'با خویشتن' = 'to himself' (internal remark). The proper name 'رشنواد' comes after and identifies the speaker; word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s452-g26: 'که' introduces the content clause ('that...'). 'این بانگ رعدست‌گر تندباد' is a compressed noun phrase in which ezāfe is omitted (poetic): 'این بانگِ رعدست‌گرِ تندباد' = 'this cry/sound of the gale's thunder.' 'رعدست‌گر' is an agentive noun 'thunderer' modifying بانگ; تندباد ('gale') further specifies the source.
segments.s452-g27: 'دگر باره' is a repetitive adverbial phrase meaning 'again'; here it modifies the past verb 'آمد' (came). 'ز ایوان' gives the source ('from the iwan/porch'), and 'خروش' is the sound/roar that came; the poetic order places the source before the sound.
segments.s452-g28: 'که' here introduces reported speech or an utterance that follows. 'ای طاق' is a vocative ('O arch') addressing the arch directly; 'چشم خرد' ('the eye of wisdom') is the definite object and is marked by 'را'. 'مپوش' is a prohibitive verb form ('do not cover/close') with the negative prefix 'م-' attached to the present stem in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s452-g29: 'که' introduces the explanatory clause. 'در تست' is an archaic expression: 'در' = 'in' and 'تست' functions as an old 2nd‑person possessive/copula ('in thee' / 'is in you'), so the whole phrase states that 'the son of King Ardashir is in you' (فرزند شاه اردشیر = 'the king's son'). Word order reflects older/classical usage.
segments.s452-g30: 'ز باران مترس' is an imperative prohibition: 'مترس' = 'do not fear' and 'ز' = 'of/from' (so 'fear not of the rain' = 'do not fear the rain'). 'این سخن یادگیر' follows as a second imperative: 'یادگیر' (learn/remember) + 'این سخن' ('these words' / 'this saying'). In classical Persian 'یادگیر' is a verbal imperative; modern dictionaries may list it differently.
segments.s452-g31: 'سیم بار' is a compound adjective 'silver‑laden' (literally 'bearing silver'), modifying 'آوازش' ('his voice'); the noun 'بار' ('load/burden') is used in a possessive/attributive sense. 'آمد به گوش' = 'came to the ear' — 'آوازش' is the theme perceived by hearing.
segments.s452-g32: 'شگفتی' (astonishment) is the subject; 'دلش' = 'his heart' with the enclitic possessive 'ش' = 'his.' 'تنگ شد' = 'became tight/constricted' (a common Persian idiom for feeling uneasy or distressed). 'زان' = 'because of that' links the result to 'خروش' (that roar), which is the cause of his constricted heart.
segments.s452-g41: به پیش سپهبد is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of / before the spahbed' (به + پیش functioning together). بگفت is a past-tense reporting verb 'he said/ordered' placed after the prepositional phrase, and آنچ دید is a relative clause 'that which he saw' (آنچ = 'that which', دید = past 'saw'). Word order is typical older Persian: prepositional phrase + verb + reported content.
segments.s452-g42: دل پهلوان is a nominative noun phrase 'the hero's heart' (poetic juxtaposition without ezāfe). زان سخن is a prepositional phrase 'from/by that speech' (زان = 'from that'). بردمید is an archaic past-form verb meaning 'was carried off/was swept away' (used here to express the heart being moved). The clause literally reads 'the hero's heart, from that speech, was carried away.'
segments.s452-g43: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past verb 'he commanded/said'. کو را here functions as an interrogative/indefinite object 'whom/whatever it is' with را as the object marker (this is an idiomatic archaic use). بخوانید is an imperative/plural or polite form 'call/summon', and زود is an adverb 'quickly/at once' modifying the verb.
segments.s452-g44: خروشی برین سان is a noun phrase meaning 'a clamor in this manner' (بر + ین (این) + سان = 'upon/like this'). که introduces a result/relative clause 'so that / that', یارد is 'companion/friend', and شنود is a classical 3rd-person form 'he hears' (here best read as resultive/subjunctive: 'so that the companion may hear').
segments.s452-g45: برفتند و گفتند is a sequence of past plural verbs 'they went and they said/called'. کای is the archaic vocative particle 'O', and خفته مرد is the vocative phrase 'sleeping man' addressed by the speakers. The clause is what they cried aloud to the addressee.
segments.s452-g46: ازین خواب= 'from this sleep' (از + ین (این) + خواب). برخیز is an imperative 'rise/stand up', و = 'and', بیدار is an adjective/verb 'awake', and گرد is an archaic auxiliary/imperative 'be / become' used to form the command 'be awakened' (broadly: 'rise and be awake'). The sequence gives two imperatives directed at the sleeping man.
segments.s452-g47: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning 'when'. دارا (Dārā) is the personal name/agent, به اسپ اندر آورد پای is a verbal phrase 'placed/put his foot on the horse' (به اسپ = 'onto the horse', اندر آورد = 'brought/put in', پای = 'foot/stirrup'); note the object (پای) follows the verb cluster in this poetic order.
segments.s452-g48: شکسته modifies پای earlier ('with the foot broken' or 'his broken foot'). رواق اندر آمد ز جای is a clause 'he entered the portico from his place' (رواق = 'portico/arcade', اندر آمد = 'entered/came in', ز = poetic 'from', جای = 'place/position'). The whole line reports his coming despite the broken foot.

sentences.s452-s29: He ordered that, when they asked for garments, they should arrange a place in the khar-gah (stall).
sentences.s452-s30: He kindled a blaze like a mountain of fire; a great quantity of agarwood, together with musk and ambergris, was burned.
sentences.s452-s31: When the sun raised its head above the mountain ridge, the spahbed prepared to depart and put his affairs in order.
sentences.s452-s32: He commanded that a mobad act as guide and that a single set of garments, from head to foot, be provided.
sentences.s452-s41: Bring Gāzar's wife, Gāzar, and Mehré; bring Bahram and Zohreh as well.
sentences.s452-s1: It was so bad that one day a gale arose, and Rashnawad became sorrowful because of it.
sentences.s452-s2: There was thunder and rain with lightning and tumult: the earth was full of water and the sky was in turmoil.
sentences.s452-s3: Because of the rain they rushed in every direction, and they set up tents within the plain.
sentences.s452-s4: There was grief because of that affair; Darab too, because of the rain, leapt/searched for a way of escape.
sentences.s452-s33: A horse, with saddle and golden trappings, and a lasso and a sword in a golden scabbard.
sentences.s452-s34: They delivered him to Darab, and (Darab) asked him, 'Who are you, lion‑hearted chief Namjuy?'
sentences.s452-s35: If you are a man, where is your native land? If it be fitting, tell the whole truth (literally: the whole right way).
sentences.s452-s36: When Darab heard, he immediately spoke and disclosed what had been hidden of the past.
sentences.s452-s17: He said to the sage, “What is this? — perhaps a body, someone who must be turned toward the arch.”
sentences.s452-s18: Look and see who lies sleeping inside, and who is thus dishevelled upon his own body.
sentences.s452-s19: They went forward and saw a young man, wise and with the face of a hero.
sentences.s452-s20: All his garments and baggage were wet and ruined, the spot made of black dust.
sentences.s452-s5: He looked and saw a ruined place; in its midst he saw an arch set on a base.
sentences.s452-s6: It was tall and ancient and was damaged; it was a royal place, full of curtains.
sentences.s452-s7: It was neither a donkey-stall nor a curtain-hall; neither a tent, nor an anbaz, nor a beast of burden.
sentences.s452-s8: On that ruined arch he stopped and slept, for he was a solitary man, friendless and without a mate.
sentences.s452-s25: When the noble king saw that marvel, he looked at Darab's cypress-like foot.
sentences.s452-s26: He said thus: 'O this wonder — wondrous indeed; from this nothing superior can thought take hold.'
sentences.s452-s27: He quickly went with him into the curtain-chamber, and repeatedly cried, 'O just God!'
sentences.s452-s28: No one in the world had seen this marvel, nor heard of it in the deeds of the great.
sentences.s452-s9: The spahbed wheeled his host about and passed by that distressed arch.
sentences.s452-s10: From the ruins a roar reached his ear; from that place the sound of the cry came to him.
sentences.s452-s11: “O distressed arch, be alert; be the guardian of this king of Iran.”
sentences.s452-s12: He had no tent, no companion or mate; he came under your (protection) and slept.
sentences.s452-s37: After that, when the woman departed, she, remembering, kept saying words to Rashnavad.
sentences.s452-s38: She took out from her chest rubies and bracelets, and from dinars and brocade at her side.
sentences.s452-s39: One by one she told the commander of the army about sleep, about rest, about food and about hiding.
sentences.s452-s40: At once she sent someone to Rashnavad; to the messenger she said, 'May it be so.'
sentences.s452-s13: Rashnavad thus said to himself that this sound was the thunder of the gale.
sentences.s452-s14: Again a roar came from the iwan: 'O arch, do not close the eye of wisdom.'
sentences.s452-s15: That the son of King Ardashir is within you — do not fear the rain; learn these words.
sentences.s452-s16: His silver-laden voice came to the ear; amazement tightened his heart at that roar.
sentences.s452-s21: He said before the spahbed what he had seen; the hero's heart was carried away by that speech.
sentences.s452-s22: He commanded, 'Quickly summon whoever it is; make such a clamor that the companion will hear.'
sentences.s452-s23: They went and called, 'O sleeping man, rise from this sleep and awake!'
sentences.s452-s24: When Dārā set his foot on the horse—his foot being broken—he came into the portico from his place.
